title: Measurement
objective: Students will explore measurement concepts including Area of Triangles & Parallelograms, Miles & Kilometres, Volume as additive and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Area of Triangles & Parallelograms? Let's find out together!
Calculate the area of parallelograms and triangles using formulae (A = b × h for parallelograms, A = ½ × b × h for triangles)
Let me show you something cool. Calculate the area of a parallelogram with base 8 cm and height 5 cm
If you is cutting triangular pieces of cloth where the base is 8 cm and the height is 5 cm, can they use a formula to work out the area of each piece?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Area of Triangles & Parallelograms: Calculate the area of a parallelogram with base 8 cm and height 5 cm Calculate the area of a triangle with base 12 cm and height 7 cm
Let's explore Miles & Kilometres! Have you ever wondered about this?
Convert between miles and kilometres using the approximate relationship (5 miles ≈ 8 km)
Time to try this out: Convert 40 miles to approximately 64 km
If you is driving 40 miles to a theme park, can they use the rule that 5 miles ≈ 8 km to estimate roughly how many kilometres that is?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Miles & Kilometres: Convert 40 miles to approximately 64 km Explain why the conversion factor between miles and km is approximately 1.6
Let's explore Volume as additive! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recognise volume as additive; find volumes of composite solid figures made of two or more non-overlapping right rectangular prisms
Let's practice together! Decompose an L-shaped solid into two cuboids and calculate total volume
If an L-shaped swimming pool is made of two rectangular sections, can you find the volume of each section separately and add them to get the total volume of the pool?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Volume as additive: Decompose an L-shaped solid into two cuboids and calculate total volume Solve a real-world problem requiring the volume of a composite figure (e.g. a step-shaped structure)
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Decimal place value!

Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given system (e.g. 5 cm to 0.05 m) using decimal notation to up to three decimal places; convert between smaller and larger units of length, mass, volume, and time
Let's practice together! Convert 3,250 g to 3.25 kg using decimal notation
If you needs to convert 5 cm to metres and write the answer as a decimal, can they do it — and also convert 2.45 kg into grams?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimal place value: Convert 3,250 g to 3.25 kg using decimal notation Convert 0.45 km to 450 m
Let's explore Measurement Conversions! Have you ever wondered about this?
Solve problems involving the calculation and conversion of units of measure, using decimal notation and multi-step reasoning in real-world contexts
Time to try this out: Solve: 'A recipe uses 0.75 kg of flour. How many grams are needed for 3 batches?'
If you is filling a fish tank that holds 60 litres and the tap fills it at 3.5 litres per minute, can they work out how long it will take to fill the tank?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Measurement Conversions. Solve: 'A recipe uses 0.75 kg of flour. How many grams are needed for 3 batches?' Compare 2.5 litres and 2,450 ml by converting to the same unit
Let's discover the amazing world of Estimating answers (age 10+)!

Find the volume of right rectangular prisms by packing with unit cubes and show it equals l × w × h (or base area × height); apply V = l × w × h and V = B × h to solve real-world problems; calculate, estimate, and compare volumes of cubes and cuboids in standard units (cm³, m³)
Let's practice together! Use V = l × w × h to find the volume of a cuboid 4 cm × 3 cm × 5 cm = 60 cm³
If you wants to work out how much soil fits in a plant box that is 40 cm long, 20 cm wide, and 15 cm deep, can they use a formula to calculate the volume?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Estimating answers (age 10+): Use V = l × w × h to find the volume of a cuboid 4 cm × 3 cm × 5 cm = 60 cm³ Explain why packing a prism with unit cubes gives the same result as multiplying edge lengths
Today we're going to learn about Perimeter (age 10+). Get ready for an adventure!
Recognise that shapes with the same area can have different perimeters and vice versa; explore this relationship systematically
Let me show you something cool. Draw two rectangles with area 24 cm² but different perimeters
If you has 12 square tiles to arrange, can they make different-shaped rectangles and notice that the area stays the same (12 tiles) but the perimeters are all different?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Perimeter (age 10+). Draw two rectangles with area 24 cm² but different perimeters Find two shapes with perimeter 20 cm but different areas
Let's explore Counting Unit Cubes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Measure volumes by counting unit cubes using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and other units
Time to try this out: Count unit cubes in a 3D diagram to determine volume in cm³
If you sees a 3-D shape built from small cubes in a diagram, can they count how many cubes it contains — even the ones hidden at the back — to find the volume?
Great job! Today we learned about Counting Unit Cubes. Count unit cubes in a 3D diagram to determine volume in cm³ Measure the volume of a small container by filling it with centimetre cubes
Let's discover the amazing world of Measuring length (age 10+)!

Recognise volume as an attribute of solid figures; understand that a unit cube (side length 1 unit) has 'one cubic unit' of volume and can be used to measure volume; a solid packed with n unit cubes has volume n cubic units
Time to try this out: Explain what 'volume' means for a 3D shape and how it differs from area
If you builds a solid shape using small cubes, can they count the cubes to find the volume — and explain what "one cubic centimetre" means in everyday terms?

Great job! Today we learned about Measuring length (age 10+). Explain what 'volume' means for a 3D shape and how it differs from area Identify a unit cube and explain that it represents one cubic unit of volume
